stories.title: Schwarber's 60-HR Club Chase — The Pace No One Has Seen Since Bonds (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 1-1
x_posts.text:
Kyle Schwarber has 20 home runs.

He hit No. 20 on May 15 — the earliest date in MLB history to reach that mark.

Through his first 45 games, nobody in MLB history hit 20 HR faster.

He's on pace for 66 home runs. 🤯 #Phillies

x_posts.label: Post 1-2
x_posts.text:
The 60-HR club has only 3 members.

Bonds (73).
McGwire (70).
Sosa (66, 65, 63).

Kyle Schwarber is currently on pace for 66.

No one has been on this trajectory since Bonds in 2001. ⚾ #Phillies

facebook.long_form:
⚾ KYLE SCHWARBER IS ON A 60-HOME-RUN PACE ⚾

Let's just say that out loud.

Kyle Schwarber hit his 20th home run of the 2026 season on May 15. That's the earliest date in MLB history that any player has reached 20 home runs in a season.

Through his first 45 games, he is on pace for approximately 66 home runs.

To put that in context: the 60-home-run club has exactly three members in the history of baseball. Barry Bonds hit 73 in 2001. Mark McGwire hit 70 in 1998. Sammy Sosa hit 66, 65, and 63 in the same three-year stretch.

No player has been on this pace since Bonds in 2001.

Schwarber returned this week after missing a few games with a GI illness and immediately homered. He looks completely locked in. His exit velocity, his barrel rate, and his contact quality all suggest this is not a fluke.

Will he sustain it? Nobody hits 66 home runs in a season easily. The odds-on outcome is regression toward something more normal — but "more normal" for Schwarber right now still means a 45-55 HR season, which would be one of the best in modern MLB history.

For now, in late May, Kyle Schwarber is on a pace that hasn't been seen since the steroid era. And he is doing it clean.

⚾ Keep watching. This is something. #Phillies

[What's your over/under for Schwarber's final HR total this season?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Kyle Schwarber hit No. 20 on May 15 — the earliest date in MLB history to reach that mark. He's on pace for 66 home runs. The 60-HR club has 3 members. Could Schwarber become the 4th? ⚾ #Phillies

article.headline: Can Kyle Schwarber Join the 60-Home-Run Club? The Historic Pace Nobody Is Talking About

stories.title: White Sox 26-24 — Murakami's Takeover and the Most Surprising Team in Baseball (Tier 1)

x_posts.label: Post 2-1
x_posts.text:
The White Sox were 9-15 in early May.

They are now 26-24.

Munetaka Murakami has 17 home runs and a .941 OPS — 2nd in MLB.

The most unexpected .500 team in baseball right now. ⚾ #WhiteSox

x_posts.label: Post 2-2
x_posts.text:
Munetaka Murakami is making a real case for AL Rookie of the Year.

17 HRs. .941 OPS. 2nd in MLB home runs.

He arrived from Japan as the biggest question mark on the White Sox. Now he IS the White Sox. ⚾ #WhiteSox

facebook.long_form:
⚾ THE MOST SURPRISING TEAM IN BASEBALL RIGHT NOW IS THE CHICAGO WHITE SOX ⚾

No, really.

The White Sox started May at 9-15. Many people (reasonably) thought this was going to be another 100-loss season for a franchise that has been rebuilding for years.

They are now 26-24.

That is a 17-9 stretch over roughly six weeks. And the person most responsible for it is Munetaka Murakami.

If you haven't been paying attention to Murakami, here's what you need to know: He is a former NPB home run champion from Japan who signed with the White Sox this offseason as a big name but a massive question mark. How would his power translate? Could he handle the best pitching in the world?

The answer has been remarkable: 17 home runs, a .941 OPS, and the 2nd-highest home run total in all of MLB behind only Kyle Schwarber.

He already holds the record for the most home runs through his first 21 MLB games by a Japanese-born player — seven, breaking Shohei Ohtani's mark of five.

This is a legitimate AL Rookie of the Year race. Murakami has earned the conversation.

And the White Sox have earned a look. At 26-24, they are a game below .500 — playing competitive baseball in a competitive AL Central. Nobody had them here.

⚾ The White Sox are real right now. And Murakami is why. #WhiteSox

[Were you sleeping on the White Sox? Be honest.] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: White Sox went from 9-15 to 26-24 — and Munetaka Murakami is the reason. 17 HRs. .941 OPS. 2nd in MLB behind Schwarber. The most surprising team in baseball right now. ⚾ #WhiteSox

stories.title: Rays-Yankees Sunday Finale — Tampa Bay's AL East Grip Tightens (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 3
x_posts.text:
Rays-Yankees was rained out Saturday. Sunday finale today.

Tampa Bay: 34-15. Best record in the AL.
New York: 30-22.

The Rays have won 4 of their last 5 vs. the Yankees in 2026. Rasmussen vs. Weathers. First pitch 1:05 PM ET. ⚾ #RaysUp

facebook.long_form:
⚾ THE RAYS ARE 34-15. LET THAT SINK IN. ⚾

Saturday's game at Yankee Stadium was rained out and pushed to a September doubleheader. Sunday's finale is the only game this series gets.

And Sunday's game is Tampa Bay (34-15) vs. New York (30-22) — which tells you everything you need to know about the AL East right now.

The Rays have built something remarkable in 2026. The best record in the American League. A 5.5-game lead over the Yankees — who themselves are playing winning baseball. Drew Rasmussen (4-1) takes the ball for Tampa Bay against Ryan Weathers (2-2) for New York. First pitch at 1:05 PM ET.

34-15 is not a lucky record. That's a pace of approximately 110 wins over a full 162-game season. Tampa Bay's organizational model — pitching depth, defensive value, analytical roster construction — keeps producing winning teams even as they operate under one of MLB's lowest payrolls.

At some point, the league has to acknowledge what the Rays are doing is one of baseball's best ongoing stories.

Yankees fans still have 30-22 and a 5.5-game deficit. That's not insurmountable. But first they'd have to win today.

⚾ Game today at 1:05 PM ET. ⚾ #RaysUp #Yankees

[Can the Yankees close a 5.5-game gap, or is this Rays' year?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Rays-Yankees Sunday finale after Saturday's rainout. Tampa Bay: 34-15 (best in AL). New York: 30-22. 5.5-game lead. Rasmussen vs. Weathers at 1:05 PM ET. The AL East race is real — or is it? ⚾ #RaysUp

stories.title: Acuña Positive Update — Braves Star Day-to-Day with Left Thumb Bone Bruise (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 4
x_posts.text:
Positive update on Ronald Acuña Jr.

Left thumb bone bruise. X-rays negative.

Walt Weiss said Acuña was trying to talk his way back into the lineup immediately after the game.

He'll be fine. Braves are 35-16. ⚾ #Braves

facebook.long_form:
⚾ GOOD NEWS ON RONALD ACUÑA JR. ⚾

On Friday night in Atlanta's 9-3 win over the Marlins, Ronald Acuña Jr. left the game in the sixth inning. The culprit: a bone bruise in his left thumb after fouling a pitch off his hand.

Saturday's update was positive.

X-rays came back negative. Manager Walt Weiss gave the day-to-day designation. And then — in the most Ronald Acuña Jr. thing possible — the star outfielder was reportedly trying to talk his way back into the lineup before the game was even finished.

If that doesn't make Braves fans feel better, nothing will.

This was Acuña's third game back after spending time on the IL with a left hamstring strain that started May 2. To come back from one injury and immediately pick up another is the kind of thing that would test any player's composure. Acuña, reportedly, was undeterred.

Atlanta is 35-16. That's the best record in the National League. They are playing great baseball right now, and Acuña's presence remains the most important individual factor in their season.

The bone bruise is not a structural issue. The X-rays being negative is the critical piece of information. Day-to-day means day-to-day — not weeks, not months.

⚾ Braves fans: breathe. He should be fine. #Braves

[Acuña or no Acuña — do you trust the Braves to win the NL East this year?] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: Positive Acuña update: bone bruise in left thumb, X-rays negative, day-to-day. Walt Weiss said Acuña was "trying to talk his way into the lineup" after the game. The Braves are 35-16 — and their most important player should be back soon. ⚾ #Braves

stories.title: Ramírez Chasing the 300-300 Club — Nine Members, History Approaching (Tier 2)

x_posts.label: Post 5
x_posts.text:
José Ramírez just recorded his 300th career stolen base.

He has 291 career home runs.

Nine more puts him in the 300-300 club — one of 8 players ever.

Kenny Lofton is the only other Cleveland player to reach 300 stolen bases. ⚾ #ForTheLand

facebook.long_form:
⚾ JOSÉ RAMÍREZ IS NINE HOME RUNS FROM ONE OF THE RAREST LISTS IN BASEBALL ⚾

Last month, José Ramírez stole his 300th career base — becoming only the second player in Cleveland franchise history to reach that milestone, joining Hall of Famer Kenny Lofton.

He has 291 career home runs.

Nine more puts him in the 300-300 club.

Here's why that matters: the 300-300 club — players who have hit at least 300 home runs AND stolen at least 300 bases in their MLB career — is one of the most exclusive lists in the history of the sport. Fewer than ten players have ever done it.

Think about what the 300-300 combination requires. You need power and you need speed. Sustained power and sustained speed. Over a career of 15+ years, without significant injury derailment. It's not just a matter of being good; it's a matter of being a particular kind of exceptional, consistently, across a long window of time.

Ramírez is 33 years old. He's still leading the AL in stolen bases in 2026. He's still hitting for power. He is a better version of himself at 33 than most players are at 28.

Nine home runs. The 300-300 club. It's closer than it's ever been for one of the most underappreciated great players in the game.

⚾ Pay attention to what Ramírez is doing. #ForTheLand #Guardians

[Is José Ramírez the most underrated player in baseball? Make your case.] 👇

---
facebook.image_caption: José Ramírez reached 300 career stolen bases in May — one of only 9 players in Cleveland franchise history. He has 291 career HRs. Nine more = the 300-300 club. One of baseball's most exclusive lists. ⚾ #Guardians #ForTheLand

x_posts.text:
Sunday MLB wrap:

🟠 Rays 34-15 vs. Yankees 30-22 — AL East finale
🟠 Schwarber (66-HR pace) vs. Guardians
🟠 Murakami and White Sox at Giants
🟠 Braves (35-16) at Nationals — Acuña day-to-day

Baseball in late May is very good right now. ⚾

facebook.long_form:
⚾ YOUR SUNDAY MLB WATCH GUIDE ⚾

Four matchups worth tracking on Memorial Day weekend:

🟠 **1:05 PM ET — Rays vs. Yankees (Yankee Stadium)**
Tampa Bay (34-15, best in AL) vs. New York (30-22). After Saturday's rainout, this is the series finale. Drew Rasmussen (4-1) starts for the Rays. Ryan Weathers (2-2) for New York. AL East stakes.

🟠 **1:10 PM ET — White Sox vs. Giants**
Munetaka Murakami (17 HRs, .941 OPS, 2nd in MLB) and a White Sox team that went from 9-15 to 26-24. Can they make it three wins in a row?

🟠 **1:10 PM ET — Phillies vs. Guardians**
Kyle Schwarber (20 HRs, on pace for 66) against a Cleveland pitching staff that has kept some of the best offenses in check. Home run watch.

🟠 **1:35 PM ET — Braves vs. Nationals**
Atlanta (35-16, NL best) on the road. Acuña is day-to-day with a thumb bone bruise — will he play? Even without him, the Braves are the best team in the NL.

Good baseball today. Get outside in the sun, but check the scores. ⚾

[Which game are you watching first today?] 👇

---
